metadata.subject: Social Studies
metadata.description: Comparative Cultures 12 examines how cultural diversity and complexity enhance understanding across societies. Students explore how belief systems, social organization, languages, and artistic expressions are shaped by and shape each other, and how geography and environment influenced the development of agriculture, trade, and increasingly complex civilizations. The course builds cross-cultural empathy and analytical skills.
metadata.general_description: A comparative study of world cultures examining how belief systems, social structures, language, environment, and artistic expression interact to create distinct and diverse human societies.

big_ideas.statement: Understanding the diversity and complexity of cultural expressions in one culture enhances our understanding of other cultures.
big_ideas.elaboration: Deep study of a single cultural tradition builds conceptual tools — about symbol, narrative, ritual, and power — that transfer to comparative understanding of other cultures.

big_ideas.statement: Interactions between belief systems, social organization, and languages influence artistic expressions of culture.
big_ideas.elaboration: Art, architecture, literature, and music are not separate from social life — they reflect and reinforce the values, hierarchies, and beliefs of their societies.

big_ideas.statement: Geographic and environmental factors influenced the development of agriculture, trade, and increasingly complex cultures.
big_ideas.elaboration: Access to rivers, fertile soil, and trade routes shaped where civilizations flourished and how they developed economically and politically.

big_ideas.statement: Value systems and belief systems shape the structures of power and authority within a culture.
big_ideas.elaboration: Whether a society is organized around ancestral hierarchy, divine right, meritocracy, or democratic principles reflects its deepest values.

categories.name: Inquiry
categories.raw: Use Social Studies inquiry processes and skills to ask questions; gather, interpret, and analyze ideas; and communicate findings and decisions.

competencies.raw: Use Social Studies inquiry processes to investigate cultures and communicate findings.
i_can_statements: I can develop a comparative inquiry question about two or more cultural traditions.
I can research cultural practices using respectful and diverse sources.
I can present my comparative findings in a structured format.
I can revise my cultural generalizations when I encounter counterexamples.

categories.name: Significance
categories.raw: Assess and compare the significance of cultural expressions at particular times and places.

competencies.raw: Assess and compare the significance of cultural expressions at particular times and places.
i_can_statements: I can explain why a specific cultural practice, artwork, or tradition is significant within its cultural context.
I can compare how different cultures mark the same universal human experience (birth, death, transition).
I can evaluate how significance is assigned differently by insiders and outsiders to a culture.

categories.name: Evidence
categories.raw: Evaluate inferences about the content, origins, purposes, context, reliability, and usefulness of multiple sources.

competencies.raw: Evaluate inferences about cultural artifacts and sources: content, origins, purposes, context.
i_can_statements: I can analyze a cultural artifact (art, tool, text) and draw inferences about the society that created it.
I can evaluate whether a source about a culture was created by insiders or outsiders, and what difference that makes.
I can identify ethnocentrism or bias in cultural analysis.

categories.name: Continuity and Change
categories.raw: Analyze continuities and changes in diverse cultures at different times and places.

competencies.raw: Analyze continuities and changes in diverse cultures.
i_can_statements: I can trace how a cultural tradition has evolved over centuries while maintaining core elements.
I can identify what causes cultures to change: contact, conquest, trade, technology.
I can evaluate whether cultural change represents loss, adaptation, or growth — depending on perspective.

categories.name: Cause and Consequence
categories.raw: Assess development and impact of thought, artistic expressions, power, and technological adaptations.

competencies.raw: Assess the development and impact of cultural thought, artistic expressions, power structures, and technology.
i_can_statements: I can trace how contact between cultures changed both cultures involved.
I can analyze how political power shapes cultural production (what art gets made, who funds it).
I can evaluate the consequences of cultural imperialism on minority cultures.

categories.name: Perspective
categories.raw: Explain different perspectives on past and present cultures.

competencies.raw: Explain different perspectives on past and present cultures.
i_can_statements: I can explain how a cultural practice that seems strange from the outside makes sense from within its cultural context.
I can analyze how colonialism distorted Western understanding of non-Western cultures.
I can compare insider and outsider perspectives on the same cultural tradition.

categories.name: Ethical Judgment
categories.raw: Make reasoned ethical judgments about actions in the past and present, and assess appropriate ways to remember and respond.

competencies.raw: Make reasoned ethical judgments about cultural actions and appropriate ways to remember and respond.
i_can_statements: I can make an ethical judgment about cultural appropriation using evidence and ethical reasoning.
I can evaluate how cultural heritage should be preserved, shared, and protected.
I can discuss what 'cultural respect' means in practice and what it requires of us.

areas.name: Definitions and Terminology
areas.raw: Definitions of culture and historical changes in terminology.
topics: Defining culture: material, non-material, and symbolic dimensions
Evolution of the concept of 'culture' in anthropology and social science
Distinguishing culture, society, civilization, and subculture
Cultural relativism vs. ethnocentrism
Multiculturalism: policy and practice

areas.name: Elements of Culture
areas.raw: Elements of culture including language, artistic expression, symbols, archetypes, materials, and techniques.
topics: Language as a carrier of culture: Sapir-Whorf hypothesis
Oral vs. written traditions
Visual art, music, dance, and literature as cultural expression
Symbols and their cultural meanings
Archetypes across cultures: the hero, the trickster, the mother figure
Material culture: tools, architecture, and technology

areas.name: Conflict and Resolution
areas.raw: Conflict and resolution within and between cultures.
topics: Sources of cultural conflict: religion, resources, identity, power
Intercultural conflict resolution: diplomacy, dialogue, mediation
Cultural assimilation, integration, and segregation
War and conquest as cultural forces
Reconciliation processes after cultural conflict

areas.name: Systems of Power, Authority, and Governance
areas.raw: Systems of power, authority, and governance.
topics: Political structures across cultures: monarchy, democracy, chiefdom, empire
Religious authority and political power
Gender and power: patriarchy, matriarchy, and gender-equitable systems
Colonial and post-colonial power structures
Indigenous governance models

areas.name: Value and Belief Systems
areas.raw: Value and belief systems in cultural development.
topics: World religions and their cultural influence
Indigenous spiritual traditions and worldviews
Secular humanism and its cultural expression
How belief systems shape law, family structure, and economics
The interplay between religion and political power

areas.name: Cultural Interactions and Exchanges
topics: Trade routes as cultural highways: Silk Road, Indian Ocean Trade
Cultural diffusion: how ideas, technologies, and art spread
Colonialism and cultural imperialism
Globalization and cultural homogenization vs. hybridization
Cultural exchange in the digital age

areas.name: Culture-Environment Interactions
areas.raw: Culture-environment interactions including adaptation, resource use, and environmental interdependence.
topics: How geography shapes cultural practices (diet, shelter, clothing, ritual)
Environmental stewardship in Indigenous cultures
Agriculture and its role in creating settled civilizations
Resource use and environmental impact across cultures
Climate change and cultural adaptation
